item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: I've updated the WikiWalk proposal accordingly.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Hmm. I feel like the link to 084 could be presented in a more scientific manner, such as the footnote stating, "The feeling that one's surroundings are not real, comparable to the anomalous effects of SCP-084". or the like. That way the link seems more organic and logical/smooth in-universe.

Of course, since I am the co-author for this, I will defer to the other co-author if they would prefer that no link be present.

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: timer expired, edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

When I read "derealization", SCP-084 comes to mind. I think these two SCPs could be linked together because they both involve reality and mind-bending buildings.

Editing this:

Extended exposure to SCP-2773-1 has resulted in many cases of inability to focus, derealization1, hyperventilation, and persistent paresthesia2.

To this:

Extended exposure to SCP-2773-1 has resulted in many cases of inability to focus, derealization3, hyperventilation, and persistent paresthesia4.

Footnotes
1. The feeling that one's surroundings are not real
2. Numbness or tingling sensations
3. The feeling that one's surroundings are not real, comparable to the anomalous effects of SCP-084.
4. Numbness or tingling sensations

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a sufferer of panic attacks myself, I heartily +1 this article of a building finally getting the help it needs.

item.title: Re: Admin Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Interesting to see people coming through and making edits as they please… But nice to see it still getting a little attention after a year and a half.

item.title: Admin Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that a recent edit was reverted, as it made unnecessary changes (removing commas) and also introduced an error.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good! Then me and Zyn (if and/or when available) will get to work on that! Thank you for your time!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm, test logs could potentially strengthen the impact of the resolution, via some escalation in testing complexity/urgency to complement the increasing radius of effect of the anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure how it would interact with other buildings unless those to had the same supernatural qualities as the original. I also feel like changing furniture would disrupt the SCP quite a lot, but I'm not entirely sure. Perhaps we could add additional testing into what was attempted as well as their results as to if they were successful or not. One thing I love about some SCP is when they test them; just reading through everything they do and what happens is actually quite enjoyable. I wouldn't mind adding this into the SCP document, so I'd like to know some feedback on this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good question. Maybe rearranging furniture, fixing up the place, or perhaps inducing some kind of interaction between it and other buildings. Granted, these ideas sound mundane as well, but then again, I'm not a particularly creative person. Honestly, I get where you're coming from, though, in the sense that a simple solution can sometimes have a kind of quiet profundity to it that is lost in the grandly complex.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What would be the more difficult, less banal route?

Part of the storyline was intended to be a nudge to the fact that sometimes the solution is relatively simple, but the Foundation often doesn't start off with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Though I initially downvoted, upon further review, I've decided to no-vote. The premise is interesting and the set-up is well-executed, but the resolution feels oddly contrived. Yeah, sure, it's a weird building that's 'experiencing' a very human condition, but the treatment of simply talking to it as if it were, well, a human, just feels too plain; like you've taken the easier, relatively banal route.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree that the idea feels like it could use a little more, maybe something as to why the building is the way it is, but I think you followed it through well enough to be entertaining, and the containment procedures were well done. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I live in America and have a very hard time with judging distances, so when dealing with meters… Yea you get the point. Thanks for the help!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hm. Not really enough substance for an upvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's immensely reassuring. Thanks for running the numbers, Scorps!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A 3 story apartment building measuring 7400 m2 would have ~ 2500 m2 of floorspace per floor. If that were square, that gives you a building that's about 50 m x 50 m- a pretty reasonable estimate for the building in the page image.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But if you do square 74 you get 5476

I'm not sure if that's how area works? 74 square meters is 74 square meters. You don't need to square it to get the "true" area.

For comparison, I Googled "small apartment" and got this result:

Studio, efficiency, and bachelor style apartments all tend to be the smallest apartments with the lowest rents in a given area, usually ranging around 300 to 450 square feet (25–45 square metres) in the United States, but considerably smaller in countries such as Japan, South Korea, and several European capitals.

74 square meters would be about twice the size of the average single apartment. Definitely not enough for an entire building?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did look this up when I put 74, but I may have read it wrong… I DO think 7400 m^2 is way too big… But if you do square 74 you get 5476, so that seems pretty big enough to me…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spoilers?
hide

The building has Panic Disorder. It's contagious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So it's a living, sweaty building that occasionally changes stuff inside it, and if you talk nicely to it, it won't make you super-panicky? Am I missing anything?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A new twist on 'sick building syndrome'. The glimpse of compassion at the end - the Foundation is cold, but can warm up when it's needed - really made it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That was a pretty deep conversation, yeah.

Also something something allegory about reaching out being an action that can make a world of difference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Derp. Two zeroes added on. Does that size seem more reasonable? I'm not an expert on average building size.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Have to say, that when I first read this in chat, I didn't exactly get it. But rereading and talking about the article with Zyn has warmed it up to me.

Its an interesting way of giving an inanimate object the symptoms of a panic attack, and effective containment through personnel enacting supportive behaviors is an additionally nice touch.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 74 m2 is incredibly small - is there a typo there? Otherwise, pretty cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! Didn't know how to fix that

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Check again? My co-author had to leave, so I uploaded the image. It should be displaying now!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Saw this in chat, absolutely loved it. Nice work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's an issue with the image.

item.title: Re: Author's Post!
item.content:encoded:
Whooooo it's posted! This has been a long time in the works, probably a couple months now, with the first draft being quite different in approach and complexity. Lots of stuff trimmed and things changed.

The image is from Wikipedia's Thermogram page, and the licensing information can be found here: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Passivhaus_thermogram_gedaemmt_ungedaemmt.png

Big thanks to everyone who helped out in chat! You people are super special awesome. :D

item.title: Author's Post!
item.content:encoded:
Hey! Okami here, and I'd just like to say first thanks for all the support and help along the way of the creation of this SCP! I would've liked to say I did it all on my own, but I am no liar and I give credit where credit is due. If not for Zyn's help with the format and clinical tone, I believe that I would've never finished and probably given up eventually. She also helped with making this more reasonable as well as get all the feedback we needed to get it to where it is now. As a result, I think she deserves more than enough to become co-author of this author. In the end, I'm happy to see my first SCP become a reality, and with it's original idea entirely intact as well! Again, thanks to everyone who helped, reviewed, or even commented on the idea post!

Team:
-Zyn

Reviewers:
Initial: Kabersch, Boogey_Man23, DreadLindwyrm, Doctor Cimmerian, MobRules, KoriZero, MrWrong, Scorpion451

First pass in chat: Jekeled, Wogglebug

Second pass in chat: minmin, Lazar, Conwell, Nusquam, LadyKatie, Decibelle, Vacuum, AbsentmindedNihilist, SpectralDragon, Lex1nat0r
